title: Release readiness audit for Muse Runtime v0
description: Final release-readiness pass for Muse Runtime v0. This audit verifies documentation accuracy, CLI/TUI/web smoke tests, build verification, quality gates, and security review before v0 release.
acceptance_criteria:
- README quickstart and provider docs match current behavior
- CLI smoke works on fake provider
- TUI/help/command surfaces are discoverable or documented
- LiveView/web route smoke is build/test-covered or manually smoke-tested if feasible
- Escript build works and CLI executable responds to non-interactive help/version/status where available
- Release packaging can be assembled or documented if not appropriate for current env
- Full gates pass (mix format, compile warnings-as-errors, mix test)
- No secrets or unsafe remote-execution defaults are exposed
notes: Blocking epics muse-1ki.4 and muse-1ki have been closed. This is the final pre-release verification.
close_reason:
Release readiness audit complete. All acceptance criteria verified:

## Files Inspected
- README.md (quickstart, provider config, CLI commands, safety model documented)
- docs/security.md (comprehensive security checklist, all items checked)
- docs/provider-roadmap.md (provider config contract documented and matches README)
- mix.exs (version 0.1.0, escript and release config correct)
- config/config.exs, config/runtime.exs (external WS disabled by default, secret key base required in prod)
- lib/muse/cli/main.ex, lib/muse/cli/release_command.ex (entrypoints working)
- lib/muse/cli/tui.ex (TUI documented with help popup)
- lib/muse_web/router.ex (LiveView route at /)
- lib/muse/execution/policy.ex, lib/muse/execution/local_runner.ex (remote execution denied, safe argv-vector execution)

## Gate Results
- `mix format --check-formatted`: PASSED
- `mix compile --warnings-as-errors`: PASSED
- `mix test`: PASSED (3605 tests, 0 failures)

## Build Verification
- `mix escript.build`: SUCCESS - generated `muse` escript
- `./muse --help`: SUCCESS - prints usage and exits 0
- `./muse --no-web` with `/help`, `/quit` piped: SUCCESS - CLI responds correctly
- `mix phx.routes`: SUCCESS - LiveView route at /, external WebSocket at /socket (disabled by default)
- `MIX_ENV=prod mix release`: SUCCESS - release assembled at _build/prod/rel/muse/
- Release `bin/muse_cli --help`: SUCCESS with MUSE_SECRET_KEY_BASE set

## Security Verification
- Remote execution denied by policy (Muse.Execution.Policy)
- Local runner uses argv-vector Port.open, no shell interpolation
- Output capped and redacted, no secrets leak
- External WebSocket disabled by default, read-only when enabled
- Production requires MUSE_SECRET_KEY_BASE (>= 64 bytes)
- .gitignore excludes _build/, muse escript, erl_crash.dump

## Limitations
- No browser-based LiveView smoke (not feasible in non-interactive audit)
- TUI mode requires ExRatatui NIF; escript mode correctly rejects --tui with clear error
- Test suite has deprecation warnings about single-quoted strings (test-only, not runtime)

## Verdict
Muse Runtime v0 is RELEASE READY. All gates pass, security model is enforced, CLI/escript/release builds work, documentation matches behavior.

title: Muse Runtime v0.2.0
description:
## Objective
Post-v0 hardening, polish, and foundation after the safe v0.1.0 release. v0.2.0 focuses on making Muse production-ready in terms of release/distribution QA, UX resilience, provider robustness, persistent session foundations, and observability.

## Context
v0.1.0 delivered the complete Muse Runtime v0 vision: read-only Planning Muse, approval gates, patch proposal/checkpoint/rollback, Coding/Testing/Reviewing Muses, real providers (OpenAI SSE/WebSocket, Anthropic, Ollama, OpenRouter), CLI/TUI/LiveView integration, session persistence, memory/restoration Muses, secure remote-execution-denied runner, and comprehensive docs.

v0.2.0 is the post-v0 hardening release. No new architectural milestones — only polish, resilience, multi-workspace foundations, and release-readiness.

## Scope
- Release/distribution CI polish (artifacts, checksums, install docs, CI automation)
- LiveView/TUI/CLI UX hardening and browser QA
- Provider/model routing UX and resilience (health, error UX, retries, external tests opt-in only)
- Persistent memory/session and multi-workspace foundations
- Remote execution approval design spike (design only unless separately approved)
- Observability, docs refresh, and release readiness

## Non-Goals
- No new Muse roles or specialist profiles
- No remote execution implementation (design spike only)
- No cloud sync or SaaS infrastructure
- No major UI redesign
- No breaking changes to the public API (Muse.submit/2)
- No MCP server/ecosystem integration
- No evaluation harness or cost tracking (backlog items)
- No autonomous shell loops or browser automation

## Safety Invariants (MUST be preserved)
- Fake provider remains the default test provider
- Remote execution remains denied by default
- Secrets never appear in prompts, logs, events, or provider debug output
- All approval gates remain enforced
- Workspace/file safety is never weakened

title: Fix PR24 LocalRunner non-zero exit handling before relying on git apply
description:
Final verification of PR24 (`muse-1ki.4.5`) found a blocking correctness/security issue in the local execution runner integration.

Evidence:
- `lib/muse/execution/local_runner.ex` returns `Result.ok(..., exit_status: status)` for non-zero process exits (currently around lines 217-223 and 257-263), so `result.status` remains `:ok` even when the command failed.
- `lib/muse/tools/patch_apply.ex` `run_git_command/2` treats any `%ExecutionResult{status: :ok}` as `{:ok, ""}` without checking `exit_status` (currently around lines 425-430).
- Therefore `git apply --check` and `git apply` failures can be interpreted as success. `PatchApply.apply_via_git/3` may then mark a patch `:applied` and checkpoint `:active` even though git rejected the patch.
- The existing PR24 test `test/muse/execution/local_runner_test.exs` asserts this non-zero-as-ok behavior, and PR18 patch apply tests do not cover a `git apply --check` failure path after approval/checkpoint creation.

Why it matters:
- Patch apply's safety contract depends on `git apply --check` failing closed before writes and on actual `git apply` failures being reported. Misclassifying non-zero exits undermines that checkpoint/approval boundary and can create false audit records.

Suggested fix:
- Either make `LocalRunner` return a `Result.error/3` (or equivalent non-`:ok` status) for non-zero exit statuses while preserving captured output, or update every caller to gate on `exit_status == 0` explicitly.
- Update `PatchApply.run_git_command/2`, `GitStatus`, and `GitDiffReadonly` to fail on non-zero exits.
- Add regression tests for LocalRunner non-zero semantics and for an approved patch whose `git apply --check` fails, asserting the patch is not marked applied and the checkpoint is not active.
acceptance_criteria: LocalRunner represents non-zero exit statuses as failed results or all callers consistently check exit_status; PatchApply treats git apply --check/apply non-zero exits as errors and does not transition patches/checkpoints to applied/active; GitStatus/GitDiffReadonly return errors for non-zero git exits; regression tests cover invalid git apply and non-zero LocalRunner exit semantics.
close_reason: Fixed: LocalRunner non-zero exits now produce Result.error (status: :error) instead of Result.ok. PatchApply.run_git_command/2 fails closed on non-zero exit. TestRunner preserves test-failure-as-verification-failure semantics. GitStatus/GitDiffReadonly already handled correctly via status: :ok pattern match. All 3605 tests pass. Regression test added for git apply --check failure not marking patch as applied.

title: Security: render canonical memory safely when artifact list fields are malformed
description: PR21 muse-avz made Memory.render/1 recursively redact values inside canonical list fields, but the renderer still assumes each field is Enumerable. A persisted canonical memory artifact whose expected list field is replaced by a tuple/scalar can crash Memory.render/1 before it renders a safe summary. Confirmed with MIX_ENV=test mix run --no-start -e using project_facts: {:password, "field-sentinel"}: Memory.render/1 raises Protocol.UndefinedError. More importantly, Exception.message(e) includes the raw tuple under a "Got value" section (e.g. {:password, "field-sentinel-3"}). CommandDispatcher.format_memory/1 rescues render failures and embeds Exception.message(e) in the /memory output, so malformed stored memory can leak raw sensitive values in the display error. Prompt.Assembler also treats canonical memory as Muse.Memory.render/1 without a rescue, so provider-bound prompt assembly can crash on malformed stored memory instead of producing a redacted/withheld summary.
acceptance_criteria: Memory.render/1 does not raise for canonical memory artifacts when any expected list field is nil, a scalar, map, tuple, keyword list, charlist, or deeply nested malformed term. Values under sensitive atom/string keys remain redacted and rendered output contains no raw sentinel secrets. CommandDispatcher /memory error paths must not include Exception.message/1 or any raw inspected malformed memory; if rendering fails, output a generic withheld message. Prompt.Assembler memory_summary for canonical malformed memory does not raise and does not expose raw secrets. Regression tests cover malformed whole-field tuple/scalar/map inputs in Memory.render/1, /memory display, and provider-bound Assembler.build/4.
close_reason: Closed

title: Security: close memory validation/rendering gaps for malformed nested terms
description: Review of muse-e49 found confirmed memory secret bypasses for malformed nested terms. Muse.Memory.validate_no_secrets/1 only treats 2-tuples as key/value pairs when the first element is an atom, so {"api_key", "plain-text-value"} currently validates as safe. Rendering/provider-bound paths also inspect tuples after generic redaction, so {:password, "hunter2"} in a canonical memory artifact or arbitrary map can appear raw in Memory.render/1 and Prompt.Assembler memory_summary content. A related render gap exists for malformed maps in artifact list fields other than :open_issues: render/1 applies term-aware redaction only to :open_issues, while other list fields are inspect-string redacted, which can partially or fully leak sensitive-key values such as %{password: "hunter two"} or %{"secret" => "abc def"}. These gaps weaken the muse-e49 guarantee that sensitive atom/string keys block or redact values regardless of value regex matching and that memory rendering is defense-in-depth safe for unsafe stored memory.
acceptance_criteria: Memory.validate_no_secrets/1 flags sensitive atom and string keys in tuple/key-value pair shapes, including nested tuples and list-contained pairs. Memory.render/1 uses recursive key-aware redaction/formatting for all memory artifact fields, not only :open_issues, and never emits raw values associated with sensitive keys in maps, keyword lists, tuples, or malformed nested terms. Prompt.Assembler memory_summary never emits those raw values for canonical memory artifacts or arbitrary map memory. The /memory command remains non-crashing and withholds or safely redacts malformed stored memory. Regression tests cover atom tuple, string tuple, nested tuple, non-open_issues map list fields with atom and string sensitive keys, arbitrary map provider-bound rendering, and /memory display behavior. Avoid dynamic atom creation for generated path/index segments while touching the recursive validator.
close_reason: All five confirmed security bypasses fixed with regression tests. See commit 7f7b931.

title: Security: harden memory artifact secret validation and rendering
description: Security review of closed PR21 (muse-1ki.4.2) found that Muse.Memory.validate_no_secrets/1 only applies regex checks to binary values and recurses maps/lists without treating sensitive keys as unsafe. Nested sensitive-key values such as %{open_issues: [%{api_key: "plain-secret"}]} or non-string/rendered values such as tuples/charlists can pass validation and later be exposed by Muse.Memory.render/1 or prompt memory layers. /memory also renders stored session memory directly via format_memory/1 without re-validating or redacting. Fix by using the shared redactor/sanitizer recursively, checking sensitive keys, normalizing non-string values/rendered output before approval, making compact/persist boundaries fail closed, and adding negative tests for atom/string sensitive keys, deep nesting, lists, tuples/charlists, and /memory display.
close_reason: Closed

title: Security: avoid atom creation from /handoff target input
description: Security review of closed PR21 (muse-1ki.4.2) found that lib/muse/command_dispatcher.ex:1656-1666 parses user-controlled /handoff args with String.to_atom/1. Any unknown target creates a permanent atom before registry validation, allowing atom-table exhaustion via repeated /handoff <unique> inputs. Fix by validating string target IDs through MuseRegistry.fetch/1 or String.to_existing_atom/1 after normalization, returning usage/not-found without creating atoms. Add regression tests for unknown handoff targets and long/unique input.
close_reason: Closed

title: Add real-browser LiveView smoke with console-error detection
description:
## Objective
Complete the browser-QA portion of muse-po4 with a deterministic Playwright/QA Kitten-compatible smoke that drives a real headless browser against the Muse LiveView UI.

## Context
muse-po4 delivered a useful HTTP/HTML smoke harness, but review found the original acceptance around browser console-error detection and keyboard focus cannot be proven without a real browser. The current docs intentionally mark Playwright as optional; this follow-up makes that missing browser layer explicit instead of relying on the HTTP smoke alone.

## Scope
- Reuse script/liveview-browser-smoke or add a companion script/task that starts Muse with MIX_ENV=smoke and MUSE_PROVIDER=fake on a non-default local port.
- Drive a headless browser non-interactively.
- Fail on browser console errors, page errors, unhandled exceptions, LiveView websocket failures, and failed critical network requests.
- Verify basic keyboard focusability/tab order for the composer and visible controls.
- Keep default mix test fake-provider-first and free of provider secrets.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] CI/dev command exists and runs non-interactively with no manual browser interaction
- [ ] The command launches/uses Muse with MIX_ENV=smoke, MUSE_PROVIDER=fake, and a non-default local port, then tears down cleanly
- [ ] The browser opens / and verifies LiveView connects without websocket/origin errors
- [ ] The run fails on console.error, pageerror, unhandledrejection, and critical failed requests
- [ ] The run verifies at least one keyboard path reaches the message composer and send control
- [ ] Documentation covers prerequisites, command, cleanup, and whether Playwright/QA Kitten is required
notes: Created by elixir-code-critic-ae373e during muse-po4 review. HTTP smoke passes, but it cannot prove no browser console errors or keyboard focus behavior.
close_reason: Implemented Playwright LiveView browser smoke. 8 browser checks pass: no console.error/pageerror/unhandledrejection, LiveView WebSocket connected, command/help discoverability in DOM, keyboard Tab reaches composer input, session/context panel ARIA markers, no visible secrets, ARIA landmarks, page loads. Script: ./script/liveview-browser-smoke-playwright. Prerequisites: npm install && npm run browser:install. Default mix test unchanged. All quality gates pass.

title: Phase 2: add executable LiveView browser QA smoke
description:
## Objective
Add a deterministic Playwright/QA Kitten-compatible browser smoke test that verifies the LiveView home page against a running Muse instance with fake provider.

## Context
muse-w23 implemented core UX accessibility and discoverability hardening, but QA Kitten could not execute browser smokes because there is no infrastructure to launch Muse or Phoenix on a non-default port, wait for readiness, and run Playwright checks. This issue tracks that executable browser QA gap.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Smoke checks verify at minimum: home page loads (HTTP 200), no browser console errors, basic keyboard-focusable input, `/help` discoverability hint visible, session panel renders with status indicators, no visible secrets in rendered HTML
- [ ] The smoke runs non-interactively in CI/dev (no manual browser interaction required)
- [ ] Default `mix test` suite is unchanged and remains fake-provider-first with no provider secrets
- [ ] Running the browser smoke is documented (command, prerequisites, cleanup)
- [ ] If QA Kitten / Playwright is not yet a project dependency, this task includes adding it or documenting the equivalent

notes: Implementation complete. Files: config/smoke.exs, lib/mix/tasks/muse.smoke.ex, script/liveview-browser-smoke. All 6 smoke checks pass: home page loads, accessibility markers, command discoverability, session/context panel, no visible secrets, keyboard focus indicators. IMPORTANT: This is HTTP/HTML smoke — it verifies server-rendered content but cannot detect browser JS runtime errors or prove keyboard focus behavior in a real browser. Real-browser console-error/focus smoke is tracked by muse-3pq (P2). HTTP smoke orchestration hardening is tracked by muse-1fx (P3). title: PR21: /memory clear ignores context session_id
description: Final PR21 audit found that lib/muse/command_dispatcher.ex routes /memory clear differently from the other PR21 session commands. dispatch(:memory_clear, args, _context) discards the caller context and computes session_id with context_session_id(%{}), so it always clears the default session instead of context[:session_id]. /memory, /memory compact, /handoff, /checkpoints, and /restore all use context_session_id(context). In any non-default session or future external/session-scoped command caller, /memory clear reports success while leaving that session's memory intact and may clear the default session unexpectedly. No production/test code was edited during the audit.
acceptance_criteria: /memory clear uses context_session_id(context) consistently with other PR21 commands; clearing a non-default session clears only that session's memory; default-session behavior remains unchanged; regression test covers non-default session routing and prevents accidental clearing of default.
close_reason: Closed

title: Security: make handoff reason redaction robust for non-binary terms
description: PR21 muse-mlp redacts handoff reason before returning the event spec, but Muse.Conductor.request_handoff/4 currently calls Muse.Prompt.Redactor.redact_text/1 directly on opts[:reason]. That function only accepts binaries. A non-binary reason such as {:password, "tuple-sentinel"} raises FunctionClauseError before a safe event spec is returned. Review command used: MIX_ENV=test mix run --no-start -e with Conductor.request_handoff(..., reason: {:password, "tuple-sentinel"}) returned {:raised, FunctionClauseError, "no function clause matching in Muse.Prompt.Redactor.redact_text/1"}. This leaves the returned-spec safety guarantee incomplete for tuple/list/map reason inputs and creates an avoidable caller crash.
acceptance_criteria: Conductor.request_handoff/4 accepts binary and non-binary reason terms without raising. Returned spec data.reason is safe to inspect and contains no raw values under sensitive tuple/map/keyword keys or secret string patterns for tuple/list/map/deep reason inputs. Safe binary reason text remains preserved. Regression tests cover tuple, keyword/list, map, and deep reason inputs and assert inspect(spec) excludes raw sentinel secrets.
close_reason: Closed

title: Security: sanitize handoff reason/context before event specs
description: Security review of closed PR21 (muse-1ki.4.2) found that Muse.Conductor.request_handoff/4 builds a user-visible event spec with reason copied directly from opts and context sanitized only with MetadataSanitizer. MetadataSanitizer is key-based and depth-limited; at depth > max_depth it falls back to inspect/1, which can include raw nested sensitive values before SessionServer's central event redaction runs. Because request_handoff/4 returns the event spec to callers, any caller logging/testing/persisting the spec before SessionServer.emit_session_event/5 can leak raw handoff reason/context. Fix by applying Muse.Prompt.Redactor.redact_term/1 or EventPayloadRedactor at event-spec construction, redacting reason as well as context, and adding regression tests that assert nested secret keys/values are absent from the returned spec itself.
close_reason: Closed

Fix by adding explicit SessionRouter/SessionServer APIs for memory, checkpoint listing, handoff request/completion, and approval-gated restore; update select_muse/2 to honor validated active_muse where appropriate; add command_dispatcher integration tests for live sessions.

title: ToolLoop patch_proposals are not propagated to maybe_capture_patch_proposal in Conductor.run/3
description:
## Summary

`Conductor.maybe_capture_patch_proposal/3` cannot be triggered through normal `Conductor.run/3` because `ToolLoop` accumulates `patch_proposals` in its result, but `finalize_with_specs/7` does not propagate them into `opts` for `maybe_capture_patch_proposal`.

## Details

1. `ToolLoop.build_result/2` returns `%{..., patch_proposals: state.patch_proposals}` — the data IS correctly accumulated.
2. `Conductor.run/3` calls `finalize_with_specs/7` with an `opts` keyword list that includes `provider_module`, `request`, `bundle`, `tool_loop_provider_state` — but **not** `patch_proposals`.
3. Inside `finalize_with_specs/7`, line 367 calls `maybe_capture_patch_proposal(result, muse, opts)`.
4. `maybe_capture_patch_proposal/3` reads `Keyword.get(opts, :patch_proposals, [])` — which always returns `[]` because `:patch_proposals` was never added to opts.
5. Result: the function always falls through to the catch-all clause on line 845 and returns `result` unchanged.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Add `patch_proposals: tool_loop_result.patch_proposals` to the opts keyword list in both call sites of `finalize_with_specs/7` in `Conductor.run/3` (the `:completed` path at ~line 264 and the `:cancelled` path at ~line 305)
- [ ] Write a test that verifies `Conductor.run/3` with a Coding Muse and tool calls that produce patch proposals correctly triggers `maybe_capture_patch_proposal`
- [ ] Verify session transitions to `:awaiting_patch_approval` after a turn with patch_propose tool calls via `Conductor.run/3`

## Files

- `lib/muse/conductor.ex` — the two `finalize_with_specs/7` call sites in `run/3` (around lines 236-265 and 264-305) need `:patch_proposals` added to the opts keyword list
- `lib/muse/conductor/tool_loop.ex` — `build_result/2` already correctly returns `patch_proposals`

## Labels

`bug`, `pr17-follow-up`, `patch-proposal`
close_reason: Fixed: added patch_proposals: tool_loop_result.patch_proposals to both finalize_with_specs/7 call sites in Conductor.run/3 (completed and cancelled paths). Regression test verifies Conductor.run/3 with Coding Muse + patch_propose tool call through ToolLoop correctly captures pending_patch and transitions session to :awaiting_patch_approval.

title: Harden LiveView HTTP smoke orchestration and option validation
description:
## Objective
Reduce false positives and unclear failures in the HTTP/HTML smoke harness delivered by muse-po4.

## Context
Review confirmed script/liveview-browser-smoke works on a free non-default port, but the script and mix task still have edge cases: the script does not fail fast if the launched server process exits during readiness, it can pass against a stale server already listening on the requested port, MUSE_BROWSER_SMOKE_HOST is used for curl/assertions but not passed to mix muse, and mix muse.smoke ignores OptionParser invalid/positional arguments.

## Scope
- Add a port/process preflight or readiness check that verifies the launched Muse process is still alive and fail clearly if bind/startup fails.
- Avoid passing against an unrelated or stale server on the requested port.
- Make MUSE_BROWSER_SMOKE_HOST behavior explicit: pass --host through, validate it, or remove the advertised override.
- Validate mix muse.smoke options, unknown flags, positional args, and port range before running assertions.
- Preserve current fake-provider/no-secrets behavior.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] script/liveview-browser-smoke fails quickly and prints server logs if mix muse exits before readiness
- [ ] The script cannot report success solely because a stale/unrelated server was already listening on the port
- [ ] Host override behavior is implemented and documented consistently
- [ ] mix muse.smoke rejects invalid ports, unknown flags, and positional arguments with clear errors

title: Phase 2: manual WCAG 2.1 AA / screen-reader audit of LiveView
description:
## Objective
Conduct a manual WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility audit of the Muse LiveView interface with screen-reader testing.

## Context
muse-w23 added programmatic ARIA attributes (roles, labels, live regions, sr-only utilities) and basic a11y marker tests in ExUnit. However, full WCAG 2.1 AA compliance requires manual testing with assistive technologies (VoiceOver, NVDA, keyboard-only navigation) that cannot be automated in ExUnit or Playwright alone. This is a genuine gap outside the executable smoke scope.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Manual audit performed with at least one screen reader (VoiceOver on macOS preferred)
- [ ] Keyboard-only navigation verified: all interactive elements focusable, no keyboard traps, tab order logical
- [ ] Color contrast ratios meet AA minimums (4.5:1 for text)
- [ ] ARIA landmarks verified by assistive tech match the roles added in muse-w23
- [ ] Audit findings documented with severity and remediation steps
- [ ] Any critical findings filed as separate bug issues

## Scope
- LiveView home page (chat panel, context sidebar, toast notifications)
- Patch proposal panel (when visible)
- Diagnostics drawer
- Session status card

## Out of Scope
- TUI/CLI accessibility (terminal-based, different accessibility model)
- Automated Playwright/axe checks (tracked in muse-po4)
notes:
QA Kitten browser/ARIA/keyboard/contrast audit completed: PASS WITH CONCERNS.

Summary:
- Automated smoke tests: PASSED
- Desktop keyboard nav, focus indicators, accessible names: PASSED
- No visible secrets, visible text mostly passed contrast: PASSED
- High: Diagnostics drawer focus management broken (focus not moved, background stays tabbable)
- Medium: Duplicate main landmarks, no rendered skip link
- Medium: Over-broad/nested live regions (log/status nesting, whole-drawer aria-live)
- Medium: Narrow/mobile composer reflow issues, hidden sidebar controls
- Low-Medium: Textarea placeholder contrast ~4.38:1 (target ≥4.5:1)

Follow-up issues filed: muse-1rq.1 through muse-1rq.5
Audit report: docs/audits/liveview-wcag-2.1-aa-2026-05-06.md

REMAINING ACCEPTANCE GAP: No actual OS screen reader (VoiceOver/NVDA/JAWS/Narrator) was used. ARIA/keyboard/contrast patterns were verified structurally only. An actual screen-reader audit is still required per acceptance criteria.

Recommended status: IN_PROGRESS / pending human screen-reader audit. Do NOT close until screen-reader testing completes.

title: v0.2.0 Phase 5 — Remote execution approval design spike
description:
## Objective
Design-only spike for remote execution approval. No implementation of remote execution capability. Remote execution remains denied by default throughout v0.2.0.

## Scope
- Threat model document for remote execution (what threats exist, what's in/out of scope)
- Approval model: what approvals would be needed before remote execution could run
- Audit events: what events would remote execution emit and how they'd be tracked
- Runner contract: what the Runner behaviour extension for remote targets would look like
- Design document produced as markdown in docs/ for review and future implementation
- Gating: a second approval (separate from patch/plan approval) required for remote execution

## Non-Goals
- No implementation of remote execution
- No SSH/VPS connection code
- No changes to existing Runner, Policy, or ApprovalGate
- No remote execution in CI/CD

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Threat model document exists in docs/ covering remote execution risks
- [ ] Approval model document describes required gates before remote execution
- [ ] Audit event schema defined for remote execution events
- [ ] Runner contract extension described (what a RemoteRunner behaviour would need)
- [ ] Design document is stored in docs/ as a reference for future implementation
- [ ] Remote execution remains denied by default (no code changes to enable it)

## Dependencies
- Depends on: muse-8ay (Muse Runtime v0.2.0 epic)
- Remote execution remains denied by default regardless of this design spike

title: Default parallel mix test can flake in atom-count Plan tests
description: Observed during PR19 post-implementation review: full default mix test failed in test/muse/plan_test.exs atom-count assertions while targeted Plan tests and full mix test --max-cases 1 passed. This suggests the atom-count assertions are sensitive to concurrent atom creation elsewhere in the async suite. Consider isolating these tests or replacing global atom-count deltas with deterministic no-dynamic-atom assertions.
close_reason: Closed

Gates: mix format --check-formatted (focused files), mix compile --warnings-as-errors, mix test test/muse/llm/transport/websocket.
close_reason: Closed

title: PR13 follow-up: add bearer command timeout option
description: BearerCommand.resolve/1 lacks a timeout option. Per PR13 acceptance criteria: 'Bearer command auth supports non-interactive timeout.' Add a :timeout_ms opt (default e.g. 10000) that uses Process.spawn + receive/after to enforce a deadline on System.cmd, preventing hanging prompts from blocking the auth pipeline.
close_reason: BearerCommand now has timeout_ms option (default 5000ms) with spawn+monitor isolation. :enoent and other process DOWN reasons mapped to safe error messages. Commit 2f9da84.

value: PR24 LocalRunner fix (muse-1ki.4.6): Non-zero exits now produce Result.error with status: :error. Callers that need test-failure semantics (like TestRunner) must explicitly handle :error with numeric exit_status to report as verification failure, not infra error.
